text: The surgeon utilizes a standard VATS approach with a high camera positioned behind the scapula. An anterior 10mm port and a posterior 5mm port are placed at approximately the seventh intercostal spaces.
The surgeon uses 5mm instruments to assess the diaphragm, with CO2 insufflation maintained at a pressure of six to eight millimeters of mercury.
The surgeon displays a floppy diaphragm with redundant tissue, clearly showing both the tendinous and muscular portions.
The surgeon places the first suture for diaphragm plication, ensuring the muscular fibers are tightly approximated above and below the stitch site.
The surgeon uses a Cavidian EndoStitch device to place a non-absorbable braided suture, accompanied by a bespoke Teflon pledger, in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs horizontal mattress sutures using the Cavidian EndoStitch device during a video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS), demonstrating precise stitching technique on the diaphragm.
The surgeon places Teflon pledges on either side of the diaphragm, which appears delicate and under strain, to prevent tearing during patient recovery.

The surgeon performs the first suture using a simple stitch technique due to the needle bending slightly, while subsequent sutures are completed using horizontal mattress stitches with the Covidien EndoStitch.

The surgeon performs a second suture on the diaphragm using a horizontal mattress technique. The diaphragm remains elevated, causing the lung to partially obstruct the view, but this obstruction decreases as more sutures are placed.
The surgeon uses a Maryland Grasper to lift and manipulate tissue, facilitating suturing around the grasper with precision.
The surgeon has placed a ten-millimeter port behind the scapula at a high position, and has performed intercostal nerve blocks along the intercostal nerves to minimize postoperative pain.

Carbon dioxide is insufflated into the chest cavity, and a 5mm port is placed on the left side while a 10mm port is positioned on the right. The surgeon uses a 10mm port for the cavitium end of the stitch, completing the third suture. A large space is visible under the diaphragm as the suturing progresses.
The surgical field shows a thin fold of tissue, with no visible abdominal organs such as the spleen or large bowel, indicating that suturing these organs is impossible in this area.
The surgeon places the fourth horizontal mattress suture in an anteromedial to posterolateral orientation, creating a long, tight line to evenly distribute tension across the tissue.
The surgeon addresses minor bleeding by suturing and tying down the affected area, noting the vascular nature of the diaphragm muscle, even when paralyzed.
The surgeon ties several knots to secure the placation lines, showing the overlapping of the two sides of the diaphragm as the sutures are placed.
The surgeon places horizontal mattress sutures, aiming for a total of eight to ten. The next suture site is clearly visible, and care is taken to maintain tension to avoid loose sutures.
The surgeon performs medial placation stitches on the diaphragm, nearing the completion of the procedure.
The video shows the final result of the diaphragm placation, with an extremely tight placation line visible, indicating the completion of the surgical procedure.
The surgeon places a twenty-french drain anteriorly up to the apex and an eighteen-french drain posteriorly, completing the procedure.
